title: Stanzas Composed At Carnac
content:
혻혻혻혻Far on its rocky knoll descried
혻혻혻혻Saint Michael占퐏 chapel cuts the sky.
혻혻혻혻I climb占폻; beneath me, bright and wide,
혻혻혻혻Lay the lone coast of Brittany.

혻혻혻혻Bright in the sunset, weird and still,
혻혻혻혻It lay beside the Atlantic wave,
혻혻혻혻As if the wizard Merlin占퐏 will
혻혻혻혻Yet charm占폻 it from his forest grave.

혻혻혻혻Behind me on their grassy sweep,
혻혻혻혻Bearded with lichen, scrawl占폻 and grey,
혻혻혻혻The giant stones of Carnac sleep,
혻혻혻혻In the mild evening of the May.

혻혻혻혻No priestly stern procession now
혻혻혻혻Streams through their rows of pillars old;
혻혻혻혻No victims bleed, no Druids bow;
혻혻혻혻Sheep make the furze-grown aisles their fold.

혻혻혻혻From bush to bush the cuckoo flies,
혻혻혻혻The orchis red gleams everywhere;
혻혻혻혻Gold broom with furze in blossom vies,
혻혻혻혻The blue-bells perfume all the air.

혻혻혻혻And o占폾r the glistening, lonely land,
혻혻혻혻Rise up, all round, the Christian spires.
혻혻혻혻The church of Carnac, by the strand,
혻혻혻혻Catches the westering sun占퐏 last fires.

혻혻혻혻And there across the watery way,
혻혻혻혻See, low above the tide at flood,
혻혻혻혻The sickle-sweep of Quiberon bay
혻혻혻혻Whose beach once ran with loyal blood!

혻혻혻혻And beyond that, the Atlantic wide!
혻혻혻혻All round, no soul, no boat, no hail!
혻혻혻혻But, on the horizon占퐏 verge descried,
혻혻혻혻Hangs, touch占폻 with light, one snowy sail!

혻혻혻혻Ah, where is he, who should have come
혻혻혻혻Where that far sail is passing now,
혻혻혻혻Past the Loire占퐏 mouth, and by the foam
혻혻혻혻Of Finist占퐎e占퐏 unquiet brow,

혻혻혻혻Home, round into the English wave?
혻혻혻혻He tarries where the Rock of Spain
혻혻혻혻Mediterranean waters lave;
혻혻혻혻He enters not the Atlantic main.

혻혻혻혻Oh, could he once have reach占폻 this air
혻혻혻혻Freshen占폻 by plunging tides, by showers!
혻혻혻혻Have felt this breath he loved, of fair
혻혻혻혻Cool northern fields, and grass, and flowers!

혻혻혻혻He long占폻 for it, press占폻 on! In vain.
혻혻혻혻At the Straits fail占폻 that spirit brave.
혻혻혻혻The South was parent of his pain,
혻혻혻혻The South is mistress of his grave.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Matthew Arnold represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Matthew Arnold's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
